observations.recommendation:
1.28. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct complete annual
physical count of all Property, Plant and Equipment; and promptly
prepare the Report thereon and furnish the Office of the Auditor not
later than January 31st of each year;
b. Instruct the General Services Office to review the Report on
Physical Count of Property Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) and
provide clear description of PPEs to aid for their proper
identification, as well as, for the correct classification of these
accounts;
c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting entries to
reclassify the asset accounts and those that did not meet the
capitalization threshold of ₱50,000.00 amounting to ₱2.892 million;
d. Direct the Municipal Accountant and General Services Office (GSO)
to continuously work together in reconciling and updating both their
records. Thereafter, make necessary adjustments on any
discrepancies noted to present fairly the balances in the financial
statements;
e. Require the GSO to institute measures for the immediate disposal of
all unserviceable properties. Once disposal is complete, the
Municipal Accountant to prepare entries to drop the unserviceable
PPE items from the books; and
f. Require the Municipal Accountant to disclose in the Notes to
Financial Statements the cost unserviceable properties.

2.18. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Accountant to:
a. Prepare a depreciation schedule for all depreciable assets acquired
before CY 2021, and ensure proper allocation of depreciation in line
with IPSAS 17;
b. Update the depreciation schedule with detailed information on PPE
to facilitate verification and analysis of relevant accounts, and
accurate tracking of depreciation expenses for each account; and
c. Reconcile accumulated depreciation account balances with the
depreciation schedule and make necessary adjustments to reflect the
correct valuation of accumulated depreciation and related accounts
in the financial statements, ensuring compliance with IPSAS 17.

3.12. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal
Engineer and other concerned Department heads in locating the
necessary documents for the unreconciled balance of ₱4.035 million,
so that proper adjustments can be made in the books to reflect the
correct balance of the account;
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the completed project,
costing ₱1.495 million from Construction in Progress to the
appropriate asset account, and recognize the corresponding
depreciation expense; and
c. Direct the Municipal Engineer to promptly gather and endorse to
the Municipal Accountant all documentary requirements for
processing of payment to the contractor upon project completion.

4.13. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the
Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to undertake the
following actions:
a. Revisit and review transactions of the previous years, exhaust all
available remedies to obtain supporting documentation, and
promptly record long-standing reconciling items;
b. Practice sending written notices to payees at least one month before
the checks become stale;
c. Prepare Journal Entry Vouchers (JEV) for necessary corrections
and adjustments for transactions that were verified; and
d. Strengthen coordination among departments to ensure the timely
submission of documents and recording of transactions in the
subsidiary ledgers for Cash in Bank-Local Currency Cash Account
(LCCA) to improve reliability and accuracy of financial reporting.

5.14. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Accountant to:
a. Exert best effort to gather documents relative to the unreconciled
and unidentified fund transfers and accordingly reconcile the
aforementioned accounts;
b. Prepare the corresponding aging of Due to NGAs, and LGUs
accounts and present the same in the Notes to Financial
Statements; and
c. Prepare appropriate adjusting entries to correct the Due to LGUs
account, amounting to ₱81,705.62.

5.15. We further recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to
refund the unused/residual balances of fund transfers or seek written
approval from the source agencies to utilize the balances for other
specific purposes.

6.27. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to record regular purchases of supplies and
materials to their respective inventory accounts and issuances
thereof shall be recorded as they take place, using the perpetual
inventory method;
b. The Municipal Accountant and the custodians of the concerned
Offices maintain the Subsidiary Ledger Cards and Stock Cards,
respectively, for each type of supplies and materials, and
periodically reconcile both records;
c. The GSO and custodians prepare monthly the RIS and RSMI/SSMI
for the issuances of supplies and materials and submit the same to
the Accounting Office for the recording thereof in the books of
accounts;
d. Concerned Offices periodically conduct physical count of inventories
and submit the RPCI to the Audit Team for verification;and
e. The Municipal Accountant to make necessary adjustments to record
the equipment, supplies, and materials—below the capitalization
threshold—issued to end-users. Prospectively, record transactions
of the same nature to appropriate inventory and expense accounts.

7.11. We recommended the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the
Municipal Accountant to undertake the following:
a. Coordinate with the recipient barangays and require the submission
liquidation reports and supporting documents, or require refund of
the amounts received if no implementation was made on the funds
transferred in CYs 2014 and 2016;
b. Ensure strict adherence to pertinent guidelines in the reversion of
unutilized LDRRMF to the General Fund after five (5) years; and
c. Prepare Journal Entry Vouchers (JEV) for necessary corrections
and adjustments for transactions to ensure the fair presentation of
the relevant accounts in the financial statements.

8.19. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor, the Municipal Engineer,
and other concerned Municipal Officials agreed to plan for appropriate
measures and strategies and make proper coordination to fast-track the
implementation and completion of the remaining PPAs in order to
maximize the benefits that can be derived therefrom.

8.20. We further recommended and the concerned personnel agreed to
ensure that all necessary activities and their related documentation for
the proposed projects are accomplished and submitted within the
prescribed timelines pursuant to pertinent DBM Local Budget
Circulars, and that the projects are completed based on the program of
works and the approved contracts.

9.18. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor, the members of the MDC,
the MPDC and other Municipal Officials concerned agreed to:
a. Ensure that priority development projects to be funded out of the
20% DF are well-planned and procurement and implementation-
ready;
b. Incorporate in the annual investment plan the specific periods to
implement the corresponding PPAs serving as reference for the
implementing offices to initiate implementation of the projects;
c. Include in the Memorandum of Agreement the timelines on the
implementation and liquidation of financial assistance to the
Barangays, and formulate plans appropriate internal control
procedures on how to effectively and efficiently monitor the
implementation of barangay projects funded by the Municipal 20%
DF; and
d. Implement the priority development projects programmed for the
year to ensure optimum utilization of the 20% DF in compliance
with the provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 series of 2020.

10.10. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
LDRRMC in coordination with the MDRRMO to:
a. Review and assess the viability of the unimplemented projects and
make necessary changes that could improve disaster preparedness
and response capabilities of the Municipality for the benefit of its
constituents;
b. Provide specific timetable for each PPA during the preparation of
the LDRRMFIP; and
c. Ensure that PPAs are well-planned- and procurement ready and
implement the same within the target implementation period to
maximize the benefits that can be derived therefrom.

11.6. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
concerned Municipal Officials to submit to the Audit Team the GAD
Agenda, annual GPB with appropriate endorsement, and the
corresponding Accomplishment Report within five days from the end of
January of the preceding year for evaluation of the implementation of
its PPAs.

12.8. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Accountant and other persons responsible for the
transactions to ensure the completeness of supporting documents prior
to payment in accordance with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445.

13.10. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct all
concerned department heads to undertake the following:
a. Regularly prepare and submit the quarterly Report on the
Information and Publicity on Programs/Projects/Activities, using
the prescribed template, to the Municipal Information Services
Office for posting and publication on all bulletin boards/signboards
located in conspicuous places within the agency premises, as well as
in the Municipality’s official website; and
b. Submit a copy of the report to the Audit Team on a quarterly basis
in compliance with the COA Circular No. 2013-004.

Review prior years’ transactions, exhaust remedies to secure documents supporting the same and immediately record the reconciling items which persisted for a long period; 

For current year and onwards, analyze and verify discrepancies/ errors and other reconciling items immediately after the preparation of the monthly BRS, and prepare JEV for corrections/ adjustments in the GL to present accurate balances of the cash in bank accounts; and

Ensure that monthly BRS of all bank accounts are submitted to the Audit Team within the prescribed period pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-011 and Section 74 of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the:

Municipal Accountant to ensure that perpetual inventory method is observed in the accounting for inventories;

General Services Officer to update the stock cards, submit the RPCI to the Office of the Auditor and pave way for any necessary adjustments in the books; and

Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to maintain the required forms, records and periodic reports for the recognition of expense portion of the inventories and to reconcile the same.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the: 

Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers for the CIP accounts, reclassify all completed projects from Construction in Progress account to appropriate asset accounts and to take up the corresponding depreciation expenses of the completed projects from the time these were completed until December 31, 2022; and

Municipal Engineer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant after project validation/ inspection so that the proper adjustments may be made to the CIP accounts.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to:

Require the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to provide the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year with the duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year to serve as the basis for the set-up of the correct RPT/SET Receivables in the books; and

Direct the Municipal Accountant to review the recording of receivables and reconcile the accounting records with the Municipal Treasurer’s reports and immediately effect the necessary corrections and adjustments.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the:

The Municipal Accountant to conduct periodic verification and monitoring of the balances of fund transfers recorded in Due to NGAs and Due to LGUs account and determine if balances are due for return/remit to the Source Agency after project completion pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013;

The Municipal Accountant to make necessary correcting and adjusting entries to the identified erroneous posting and to update the balances for implemented and refunded amounts relative to the Due to NGAs and Due to LGUs accounts in order to reflect the correct balances at year end; and

The concerned officers to take necessary actions to implement the unutilized fund transfers and periodically update the status of projects with the accounting unit.

We recommended that the Municipality observe the schedules or timelines set in the Memorandum of Agreement.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and other concerned officials agreed to put forth greater efforts to fill the empty mandatory positions in the LDRRMO so that programs for disaster risk reduction management will be effectively and efficiently delivered.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure: 

Strict compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 in recording and booking-up of transactions regarding the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF); and

That all booked transactions are backed-up with actual financial transactions with appropriate supporting documents.

We recommended and the Municipality agreed to make proper charges out of the DRRM fund taking into consideration the guidelines regarding the allocation and utilization of the fund provided for in DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 and other pertinent guidelines for LDRRMF for a more developed and improved disaster preparedness and response capabilities.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer and other officials concerned to submit the unaccounted DVs and its supporting documents within 15 days from receipt of this AOM.

We further recommended that Management identify other unserviceable properties for disposal so that corresponding adjustment could be made in the books of accounts, in compliance to Section 59 and Annex 51 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units, Volume II.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the:
Municipal Accountant to reclassify all completed projects from Construction in Progress account to appropriate asset accounts and to take up the corresponding depreciation expenses of the completed projects from the time these were completed;
Municipal Accountant to compute for the amount of liquidated damages for the two remaining abandoned projects; and
Municipal Engineer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant after project validation/ inspection and periodically reconcile projects status with the accounting records, so that the proper adjustments may be made to the CIP accounts.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to revert to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund any unliquidated balance of accounts payable in the books which has been outstanding for two years or more where no actual claim, administrative or judicial, has been filed or which is not covered by perfected contracts on record.
We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
review the Accounts Payable records to verify their validity;
b.	send confirmation letters for balances with identified claimants and pay those with valid claims; and
c.	adhere strictly to COA Circular No.2015-009 in the use of the prescribed accounts in the recording of transactions to ensure accuracy and reliability of the accounts reported in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
a.    	Assess the necessity of those unexpended fund balances. In the event that it is determined that the said funds are no longer needed, they must be returned to the source agencies in accordance with current laws and regulations. If necessary, management ought to ask the source agencies for permission to use the money for other related projects of the Municipality;
b.    	File requests for authority to write-off dormant fund transfers, if warranted, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2006; and
Prepare adjusting/correcting entries for the inadvertent refund in the Due to LGUs account.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to:
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to conform with the guidelines outlined in Section 20, Volume I of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs) in setting up the collectibles at the beginning of the year; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to thoroughly review the recording of receivables and reconcile the accounting records with the Municipal Treasurer’s reports and immediately effect the necessary corrections and adjustments.

We recommend to the Local Chief Executive (LCE) to direct the concerned officials to:
To ensure that the mandatory positions in the LDRRMO be filled so that programs for disaster risk reduction management will be more adequately prepared and the Municipality can achieve maximum capacity in terms of disaster prevention, mitigation, preparedness, response and recovery;
Ensure strict adherence with COA Circular No. 2012-002 in recording and booking-up of transactions regarding the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF); and
Strengthen internal controls over financial transactions and fund management to prevent errors and discrepancies and establish mechanisms for regular monitoring and reporting on the utilization of funds allocated for disaster risk reduction and management resulting to an accurate and reliable financial statement.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to:
Direct the concerned official to submit the certified copy of the transmittal letter of the liquidation report of the previous cash advance; and henceforth,
Strictly adhere to the provisions of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 on the grant of cash advance and liquidation of confidential fund.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the following:
BAC to revisit the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 9184, especially on the rules of observers and strictly comply with the said section; and
Municipal Accountant to submit the above lacking supporting documents to substantiate the claim.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Local Development Council and concerned municipal officials to formulate plans on how to effectively and efficiently monitor the implementation of the barangay projects funded by the Municipal 20% DF so that constituents could avail of vital services and benefits that could be derived therefrom.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to make sure that fund transfers from source agencies are accompanied by properly signed Memorandum of Agreement, ensure compliance to the said MOA, and see to it that the funds are exclusively used for the stated purpose.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer to:
Adhere to the posting guidelines outlined for the Notice of Delinquencies and utilize all feasible methods to enhance collection efforts, striving to meet the targets set by the Bureau of Local Government Finance (BLGF);
Compile a certified list detailing all outstanding real property tax delinquencies, including explanations for non-collection, and present this to the Sanggunian for assistance in enforcing collection remedies.
Ramp up their “Pulong-pulong” campaign to encourage payment of tax dues and educate the taxpayers on the benefits of early payment and their obligations, deadlines, and consequences of non-compliance; and
Implement a system for regular monitoring and evaluation of tax collection efforts to assess effectiveness, identify areas for improvement, and track progress toward higher collection capacity.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Officer (MENRO) and all other officials concerned to finalize the Municipality’s Safe Closure and Rehabilitation Plan, and to implement the recommendations given by the DENR-EMB to meet the objectives of the ecological solid waste management program embodied in the provisions of RA No. 9003.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Instruct the MENRO to expedite the processing of the required permits and certifications or ECC to operate the Ultra High-Temperature Pyrolysis System so that its harmless effects on the environment can be assured; and
Direct the concerned officers to properly plan and implement projects that pass the required government authority to operate in order to avoid wastage of public funds.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the concerned officials to ensure full compliance with the necessary documents before the end of the year for payment to suppliers/ creditors to avoid unnecessary build-up of Accounts Payable balance.

We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to:

Send confirmation letters for balances with identified claimants or creditors and process the payment with valid claims;

Make necessary correcting entries pertaining to erroneous posting to the Accounts Payable account; and

Exert efforts to verify the status of unclaimed payments for stale checks so that proper payment to valid payees can be made.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive, the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board and all other officials concerned agreed to implement the Municipality’s Safe Closure and Rehabilitation Plan, incorporating revisions if any, as well as recommendations given by the DENR-EMB in compliance with the provisions of RA No. 9003. 

Further, we recommended the improvement of the Municipality’s Solid Waste Management Facility and waste minimization measures to promote solid waste avoidance and volume reduction from source.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the MENRO and the Municipal Treasurer:

To make necessary measures to implement collection of garbage fees according to the prescribed rates provided on the Municipal Revenue Code for residential establishments; and

To formulate procedures incorporating internal controls on the collection of fees by the designated Eco-Aide and the remittance of the same to the MTO.

We recommended that the municipal officials and employees who received the extra cash gift for CY 2008 should be required to immediately refund the same.

Compliance with Section 325 (a) of RA 7160 and LBC No. 75 is enjoined to avoid future suspensions/ disallowances in audit.

Absence of required supporting documents prescribed under Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB) Circular No. 01-2020 was noted in the Municipality’s procurement of certain goods to address the Covid-19 situation hence, the legal, technical and financial capability of the supplier, distributor or manufacturer with whom the agency have negotiated could not be ascertained.
observations.managementAction:

Submit the required documents such as Mayor’s Permit/Business Permit;

Submit the complete list of Locally Stranded Individuals and Returning Overseas Filipino Workers including their period of quarantine to the office of the Auditor; and

Instruct the officials concerned to see to it that the necessary documents to support the claims be thoroughly prepared and attached to the disbursement vouchers.

observations.recommendation: The Municipality made inappropriate charges against the five percent Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF), contrary to the guidelines embodied in DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1, thereby precluding the efficient utilization of the fund with the objective of strengthening and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities of LGUs at all levels.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Municipality formulate DRRM programs, projects and activities and make charges from the 5% DRRM fund that are congruent with the programs for Quick Response and Disaster Prevention and Mitigation, Preparedness, Response, Rehabilitation and Recovery as embodied in NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 and other related guidelines on the utilization of the LDRRM Fund.
observations.reason: Inappropriate charges were still made in the Current Year

observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the end users to rationalize the need for consultants prior to approving the request for procurement.
observations.reason: There is still hiring of consultants without complying with RA 9184.

The titling of the municipal lots has long been overdue, and inaction is not an option. He should be directly responsible and accountable to the Municipal Mayor for all the documentary requirements and safekeeping of real property records. This will preclude the occurrence of disputes over the use of the property. The properties will be safeguarded against possible loss or intrusion of unlawful occupants/squatters.

The Municipal Accountant should take action to record, reconcile and make necessary adjustments in the books of accounts relative to the parcels of land owned by the municipality.

No action from the Management

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the:
Municipal Accountant and the GSO to conduct periodic reconciliation of the accounting and property records with the physical count and make the necessary adjustments on any discrepancies noted, for accurate and fair presentation of PPE account balances in the financial statements; and
GSO to properly fill out the details in the RPCPPE, conduct inventory of all unserviceable properties and prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) to facilitate and expedite the disposal of all unserviceable properties. Moreover, for the GSO to maintain and update the PCs to record all procurement and disposals of PPEs so that a reconciliation between the PCs with the physical count can be made.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the:
Municipal Engineer to validate projects with no details/schedules and issue Certificate of Completion of the validated projects to be used as basis by the Municipal Accountant in the reclassification to their PPE account;
Municipal Engineer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant after project validation/inspection so that the proper adjustments may be made to the CIP accounts; and
Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers for the CIP accounts, reclassify all completed projects from Construction in Progress account to appropriate asset accounts and to take up the corresponding depreciation expenses of the completed projects from the time these were completed until December 31, 2021.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Review prior years’ transactions, exhaust remedies to secure documents supporting the same and immediately record the reconciling items which persisted for a long period;
Enroll all the Municipality’s accounts under the LBP weAccess facility inorder to obtain copy/ies of bank statements at the earliest possible time; and
Submit to the Auditor the monthly BRS of the three Cash in Bank-LCCA SL balances without BRS, and henceforth ensure that monthly BRS of all bank accounts are submitted to the Audit Team within the prescribed period.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to Management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-001 dated March 29, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to:
Direct the BAC to strictly comply with the procurement law so that proper procedures will be made on purchase of goods and services; and
Ensure that all procurement transactions of the Municipality be undertaken through competitive bidding or any alternative modes of procurement as provided under RA 9184.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to Management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-009 dated May 127, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant and other officials concerned to submit the lacking supporting documents.
We likewise recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to see to it that all subsequent disbursements for infrastructure assets are duly supported with complete documents.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to Management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-011 dated June 6, 2022.

We recommended and the Management agreed to make proper charges out of the DRRM fund taking into consideration the guidelines regarding the allocation and utilization of the fund provided for in DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 and other pertinent guidelines of DRRM.
Likewise, we recommended and the Management agreed to formulate and implement DRRM programs, projects and activities that are congruent with the thematic areas and other policies of disaster risk reduction and management to strengthen and enhance the Municipality’s disaster capabilities at all levels.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to Management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-006 dated May 25, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and other concerned officials agreed to organize and fill-in the minimum required position classification and compensation scheme in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office as prescribed by NDRRMC, DILG, DBM and CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1, so that programs and projects pertaining to DRRM will be carried out efficiently and effectively.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to Management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-002 dated April 29, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the following:
a.	The Municipal Accountant to conduct periodic verification and monitoring of the balances of fund transfers recorded in Due to NGAs account and determine if balances are due for return/remit to the Source Agency after project completion pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013; and
b.	The concerned officers to take necessary actions to implement the unutilized fund transfers and periodically update the status of projects with the accounting unit.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to Management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-010 dated June 3, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the concerned officials and employees to strictly comply with the guidelines set forth under LBC No. 125 to ensure the proper and judicious application of government resources.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to Management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-004 dated May 24, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to assess the need to engage the services of consultants and stop the hiring of consultants whose functions are not highly technical or specialized in nature and whose functions are redundant to the functions of existing employees.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to Management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-007 dated May 27, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct LGU officials and employees to exercise prudence, austerity, and restraint in the use of the Municipality’s resources in conducting training workshops especially in light of the current state of the country.

Prepare and submit the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances together with the supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: Utilization of the 20% Development Fund (DF) with a total appropriation of ₱81.661 million for the year was not optimized contrary to DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2017-1, thus, depriving the constituents of vital services and benefits that could be derived therefrom.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct:
	
The Local Development Council, the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and other municipal officials concerned to formulate plans on how to effectively and efficiently implement the identified projects funded by the 20% DF and regularly monitor the status of implementation thereof;

The concerned officials to evaluate the necessity and propriety of the unimplemented PPAs so that it can be utilized to fund other identified priority development PPAs in accordance with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017; and

The concerned officials to ensure that all PPAs included in the budget for 20% DF should be in accordance with Section 3.0 of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 and shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the municipality.
observations.reason: Status of Implementation is not regularly monitored.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Accountant did not establish at the beginning of the year the Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax Receivables contrary to Section 20 of the New Government Accounting System for LGU’s Volume I, thus understating the balances of the asset accounts Real Property Tax Receivable and Special Education Tax Receivable and liability accounts Deferred Real Property Tax and Deferred Special Education Tax.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer using the assessment roll as input, update the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card and furnish the Municipal Accountant with a duly certified showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year and every beginning of the year thereafter.
observations.reason: On-going updating

observations.recommendation: Wages of municipal laborers in the total amount of ₱0.799 million was invalidly charged against the LDRRMF contrary to NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC              No. 2013-1, hence are considered to be expenses that are contrary to law, rules and regulations and as such are valid subject of audit disallowance.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that to avoid audit disallowance, the Municipal Officials concerned should ensure that only those allowable expenses are charged against the LDRRMF.
observations.reason: Expenses for wages of laborers are still charged against LDRRMF

observations.recommendation: Purchases of diesel fuel at an unusually high price contravened the state policy of promoting good governance in all its branches and departments and departing from the governing principles of government procurement of transparency, competitiveness and system of accountability as provided under the pertinent provisions of RA No. 9184 thereby putting the Municipality at a disadvantage since ₱122,909.22 was paid in excess of the average retail prices.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the BAC, other official concerned and the Municipality’s management as a whole simplify the procedure in the procurement of fuel, by adopting GPPB Resolution No. 5-2018 dated May 18, 2018 and/or such other mode allowed under RA No. 9184 and its 2016 Revised IRR, making sure that the purchase price will be the most advantageous to the government and reflective of the prevailing retail pump price.
observations.reason: Procurement for fuel not in full compliance with RA 9184

observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer and other officials concerned to submit the unaccounted DVs and its supporting documents within 15 days from receipt of this AOM.

We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to develop a system that would prevent similar occurrence in the future to avoid suspension and/or disallowance of transactions.

No. 7160, thus depriving the Municipality of additional revenues which could have been utilized for its development programs and projects beneficial to its constituents.

observations.recommendation: The Municipality have not established a formalized GAD monitoring and evaluation system required under PCW, DBM, DILG and NEDA JMC No.       2013-01 as amended, hence there is no readily available data on the progress of GAD activities, diminishing the Municipality’s capacity to ensure success of those activities.

The established/ strengthened GAD M&E system should among others incorporate mechanism to monitor the progress of the PPAs, document difficulties/problems encountered in the implementation and the solutions/remedial measures implemented.  The monitoring system should also be able to regularly report/produce data on the implementation of the Municipality’s GAD activities
observations.reason: There is delay of implementation due to COVID-19 pandemic

observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit the RCDs, RCIs, RDs and other reports to the Municipal Accountant on a weekly basis so that the preparation of JEV needed for the recording of the transactions be made accordingly to facilitate the preparation of financial statement and other reports and its submission within the reglementary periods to ensure compliance and relevance of data for various purposes.
observations.reason: Reports are not religiously submitted weekly.

Implementation of some Bottom up Budgeting (BuB) projects were not completed in accordance with the timelines set in the Memorandum of Agreement entered into by the Municipality and the fund source agency.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the:

Recommendations
We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the Municipal Accountant to properly record the purchases of welfare goods in the appropriate inventory accounts and the consumption thereof as expense, in accordance with IPSAS 12, Volume I;
Direct the concerned officials and employees to submit the Requisition and Issue Slips and Relief Distribution Sheets indicating actual issuance and distribution of the welfare goods; and
Henceforth, comply with pertinent provisions relative to the receipt, recording, issuance and distribution of inventories held for distribution.

Recommendations
We recommended and Management agreed to direct the:
Concerned officials to strictly comply with all policy guidelines and regulations in the implementation of programs, projects and activities, and utilization of funds received for specific purposes to ensure the proper and judicious application of resources and to avoid disqualification on future projects and programs of the National Government; and
Municipal Accountant to verify the completeness of the attached supporting documents before payment is made and cause the submission of the lacking supporting documents.

Recommendations
We recommended and Management agreed to:
Take heed of the basic controls in the receipt, recording, inventory, and distribution of the donations received; and
Prepare and submit the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances together with the supporting documents.

Recommendations
We recommended and Management agreed to:
Submit the required documents such as Mayor’s Permit/Business Permit;
Submit the complete list of Locally Stranded Individuals and Returning Overseas Filipino Workers including their period of quarantine to the office of the Auditor; and
Instruct the officials concerned to see to it that the necessary documents to support the claims be thoroughly prepared and attached to the disbursement vouchers.

Recommendation
We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipality formulate DRRM programs, projects and activities and make charges from the 5% DRRM fund that are congruent with the programs for Quick Response and Disaster Prevention and Mitigation, Preparedness, Response, Rehabilitation and Recovery as embodied in NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 and other related guidelines on the utilization of the LDRRM Fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct:
The Local Development Council, the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and other municipal officials concerned to formulate plans on how to effectively and efficiently implement the identified projects funded by the 20% DF and regularly monitor the status of implementation thereof;

a. Copy of the approved contract (with all its integral parts necessary for the review)

 1.Specific condition to justify resort to design and build scheme

 1.The responsibility of the contractor for the preparation and submission of all necessary detailed engineering investigation, surveys and designs

  3.Provision on the review, order of rectification and approval/disapproval of the submitted plans

   request for procurement.


   The aforementioned  audit  observation  was  communicated  to  management
   through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2020-005 dated March 24, 2020.

Recommendations


   We recommended that the Local Chief Executive  conduct  an  investigation
   and take appropriate action(s) on the noted usually high  purchase  price
   per liter of the diesel fuel purchased from April 2018 to May 2019.


   We also recommended that  the  BAC,  other  official  concerned  and  the
   Municipality’s management as  a  whole  simplify  the  procedure  in  the
   procurement of fuel, by adopting GPPB Resolution No. 5-2018 dated May 18,
   2018 and/or such other mode allowed  under  RA  No.  9184  and  its  2016
   Revised IRR, making sure  that  the  purchase  price  will  be  the  most
   advantageous to the government and reflective of  the  prevailing  retail
   pump price.


   The aforementioned  audit  observation  was  communicated  to  management
   through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2020-009 dated March 24, 2020.

Recommendations


   We recommended that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  direct  the  Municipal
   Treasurer and other officials concerned to submit  the  unaccounted  DV’s
   and its supporting documents within 15 days from receipt of this AOM.


   We  further  recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  direct  the
   Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to develop a system that
   would prevent similar occurrence in the future to avoid suspension and/or
   disallowance of transactions.


   The aforementioned  audit  observation  was  communicated  to  management
   through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2020-003 dated January 24, 2020.

Recommendation


   We recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  instruct  the  Acting
   Municipal Treasurer to enhance efforts to  collect  the  delinquent  real
   property taxes, otherwise, avail of the  remedies  provided  for  in  the
   Local Government Code.


   Management Comment


   The total amount of uncollected real property taxes  as  indicated  above
   include those imposed upon the following classified real properties which
   should have been excluded and should have been deducted from the over-all
   total collectibles and omitted from the rolls:


        a.  Timber and forest land – classified exempt pursuant to Sec.  234
           a of RA 7160; composed of approximately twenty two (22) barangays
           with a total land area of 3,646.5731  hectares  and  an  assessed
           value of ₱108.195 million.


        b. University of the Philippines Visayas, acquired properties – also
           classified exempt by virtue of R.A. 9500 with a total  land  area
           of approximately 1,200 hectares.
   The data on tax collectibles is largely dependent on the  total  assessed
   value of  real  properties  provided  by  the  Office  of  the  Municipal
   Assessor.  These data also comprises one of the bases for computation  of
   annual performance targets for treasury offices to meet.  As  such,  data
   must be thoroughly cleansed in order to obtain an accurate as well as  an
   objective target.


   Further, a dialogue with the Municipal Assessor was conducted and matters
   regarding  the  real   property   assessment   records   were   addressed
   specifically in its urgent need to purge and update  the  existing  data.
   Through these efforts, management was able to realize the  importance  of
   maintaining an updated and cleansed records in  order  to  come  up  with
   reports that are truly credible and reliable  documents.   Likewise,  the
   Municipal Assessor was instructed to submit annually the assessment  roll
   to the Municipal Treasurer’s Office as basis for the computation  of  the
   total amount due and collectible for the year.


   Auditor’s Rejoinder


   We commend the efforts  of  the  management  in  the  collection  of  the
   delinquent real property taxes as well as their thrust  in  cleaning  and
   updating the real property tax records.


   We also find acceptable the explanation of the management  that  some  of
   the delinquent taxes pertain  to  those  exempted  real  properties  that
   should have been excluded from the rolls.


   However, since our audit observation was anchored on the current  records
   of the municipality, pending the submission of the cleansed  and  updated
   records, we maintain our audit recommendation.


   We look forward that the real property  tax  records  maintained  by  the
   Municipal Assessor, Municipal  Treasurer  and  the  Municipal  Accountant
   would be reconciled.

Recommendations


   We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:


        a. Instruct the HRMO and the different department  heads  concerned
           to indicate  the  specific  duties  and  responsibilities  to  be
           performed  by  each  job  order  personnel  in  the   job   order
           form/contract.


        b. Instruct the department heads concerned to make  sure  that  the
           duties and responsibilities to be performed  by  each  job  order
           personnel   coincide   and/or   relate   to   their   appropriate
           funding/charges.


   Management Comment


   The Office of the HRMO was  tasked  to  prepare  appointments  and  other
   documents  related  to  the  issuance  of  the  job  order  appointments.
   Furthermore, the format for the said appointment was taken  by  the  HRMO
   from the Civil Service Commission and there  was  no  indication  of  the
   duties and responsibilities.  The said format was used  by  the  LGU  for
   many years since then.  However, as per observation, statement of  duties
   and responsibilities is a requisite in the issuance  of  appointment  for
   job order personnel.  Therefore, in compliance, the HRMO  will  attach  a
   job description to every issuance of appointment  and  will  require  the
   Office  concerned  to  provide  the  duties  and   responsibilities   and
   responsibilities needed in the said JO appointment.


   In compliance, the HRMO will require Heads of Offices to allocate  budget
   for JO personnel corresponding to the need of their offices and  to  make
   sure that the duties and  responsibilities  are  in  alignment  with  the
   funding/charges.

Recommendations

   amended in GAD Planning and Budgeting.

   GFPS-TWG and concerned officials should ensure that the GAD related  PPAs

   Executive Budget submitted to the sanggunian for enactment.


   The aforementioned  audit  observation  was  communicated  to  management
   through Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM) No. 2020-001 dated January  2,
   2020.

Recommendation


   We   recommended   that   looking   forward,   in   the    crafting    of
   performance/success     indicators,     the      same      should      be
   quantifiable/measurable.  This would ensure that the progress/success  of
   GAD activities could  be  clearly  established  and  the  allocation  and
   utilization of resources would be rationalized.


   The aforementioned  audit  observation  was  communicated  to  management
   through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2020-001 dated January 2, 2020.

Recommendation


   We recommended that the LCE establish the Municipality’s GAD M&E Team and
   formalize the monitoring and evaluation of  its  GAD  related  activities
   through  the  issuance  of   an   appropriate   Executive   Order.    The
   established/strengthened GAD M&E system should among  others  incorporate
   mechanism   to   monitor   the   progress   of   the    PPAs,    document
   difficulties/problems  encountered  in   the   implementation   and   the
   solutions/remedial measures implemented.  The  monitoring  system  should
   also be able to regularly report/produce data on  the  implementation  of
   the municipality’s GAD activities.


   The aforementioned  audit  observation  was  communicated  to  management
   through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2020-001 dated January 2, 2020.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the following: 1. The Local Chief Executive should look into the procedure/practices of the BAC in evaluation and post qualification of bids to determine why the bids passed bid evaluation and post qualification despite non-compliance with the technical specification set by the municipality. Also, the procedures in the preparation of contracts/purchase orders, should be reviewed to determine why the terms and conditions therein did not follow the terms and conditions/specifica tions of the municipality as presented in the Purchase Request and bidding documents. Lastly, the Local Chief Executive should implement corrective measures to prevent similar occurrences in the future. 2. The BAC should be thorough in their evaluation and post qualification of bids keeping in mind the non-discretionary “pass or fail” criteria espoused by RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR. 3. The concerned municipal official/employee charged with the preparation of contracts/purchase order should ensure that the terms and conditions/specifica tions therein are consistent with that of the Purchase Request and the Bidding Documents. 4. The Members of the BAC, TWG and BAC Secretariat and other municipal officials/employee who has participation in the procurement process should familiarize/constant ly update themselves with the provisions of Republic Act No. 9184, its Revised Implementing Rules and other procurement rules and regulation issued by te GPPB and other government agencies. 5. We further recommended that the BAC exert more effort in publicizing the bidding for the procurement needs of the municipality so that more suppliers will be able to participate. This is in line with one of the governing principle of government procurement which is competitiveness, by extending equal opportunity to enable private contracting parties who are eligible and qualified to participate in public bidding. Also, with the presence of more suppliers, the municipality will be able to obtain the best possible price and best value for its money.
